hypothesis: Combining multiple orthogonal alpha factors (value, momentum, quality) into a composite ranking creates more stable alpha than any single factor. Sector-neutral long-short construction hedges market risk and isolates stock-specific alpha.
universe: S&P 500 or Russell 1000 (liquid, large-cap for borrowability)
entry_logic: Rank all stocks by composite factor score. Long top decile, short bottom decile. Rebalance weekly or monthly.
exit_logic: Positions exit when they fall out of the top/bottom decile at rebalance.
position_sizing: Equal-weight within each leg. Dollar-neutral (equal long and short exposure). Sector-neutral (equal sector weights in long and short legs).
risk_management: Max single position: 2% of portfolio. Sector exposure < 5% net. Leverage capped at 2x. Track factor exposure to ensure no unintended concentration.
known_failure_modes: Factor crowding: when too many quants use the same factors, alpha decays.
Short squeezes: bottom-decile shorts can spike violently.
Value traps: cheap stocks can stay cheap or go to zero.
Requires fundamental data (not just price) — adds complexity and cost.
Transaction costs on weekly rebalance with 200 positions can eat alpha.

historical_performance.notes: This was the archetype strategy that won Quantopian contests. Performance degraded post-2018 as factor crowding increased. Requires universe of 500+ stocks to work.

references: Stefan Jansen - ML for Algorithmic Trading, Chapter 4-6
